content:
# Leviticus Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59v–60r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_005_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_005_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 89–90 (pa_0089–pa_0090)

---

**1** "If anyone sins in that he hears a public adjuration to testify, and though he is a witness — whether he has seen or has come to know of it — yet does not speak up, he shall bear his iniquity.

**2** Or if anyone touches any unclean thing, whether the carcass of an unclean wild animal, or a carcass of unclean livestock, or the carcass of unclean swarming creatures, even though it is hidden from him, and he is unclean and becomes guilty —

**3** or if he touches human uncleanness, whatever the kind of uncleanness might be, with which one becomes unclean, and it is hidden from him, and he then comes to know of it, and he becomes guilty —

**4** or if anyone swears, uttering rashly with his lips to do evil or to do good, whatever it is that a man utters rashly in an oath, and it is hidden from him, and then he comes to know of it, and he becomes guilty in any one of these —

**5** when one becomes guilty in any of these things, he shall confess the sin he has committed.

**6** And he shall bring his guilt offering to the LORD for the sin that he has committed: a female from the flock, a lamb or a goat, as a sin offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin.

**7** But if he cannot afford a lamb, then he shall bring to the LORD as his guilt offering for the sin that he has committed two turtledoves or two young pigeons: one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering.

**8** He shall bring them to the priest, who shall offer first the one for the sin offering. He shall wring its head from its neck but shall not sever it.

**9** And he shall sprinkle some of the blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar, while the rest of the blood shall be drained out at the base of the altar; it is a sin offering.

**10** And the second bird he shall offer as a burnt offering according to the prescribed rule. So the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven.

**11** But if he cannot afford two turtledoves or two young pigeons, then he who sinned shall bring as his offering a tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering. He shall put no oil on it and shall place no frankincense on it, for it is a sin offering.

**12** And he shall bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take a handful of it as its memorial portion and burn it on the altar, on the fire offerings to the LORD; it is a sin offering.

**13** So the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin that he has committed in any one of these things, and he shall be forgiven. The remainder shall belong to the priest, as with the grain offering.'"

**14** The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**15** "If anyone commits an act of unfaithfulness and sins unintentionally against the holy things of the LORD, he shall bring to the LORD as his guilt offering a ram without blemish from the flock, valued by you in silver shekels, according to the shekel of the sanctuary, as a guilt offering.

**16** He shall also make restitution for whatever holy thing he has sinned against, and shall add a fifth to it and give it to the priest. And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering, and he shall be forgiven.

**17** If anyone sins, doing any one of the things the LORD has commanded not to be done, though he did not know it, then he realizes his guilt, and he shall bear his iniquity.

**18** He shall bring to the priest a ram without blemish from the flock, valued in silver, as a guilt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him for the error that he committed unintentionally, though he did not know it, and he shall be forgiven.

**19** It is a guilt offering; he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 59v–60r.** Both manuscript pages were examined directly. The first page (folio 59v) contains the opening of Leviticus 5, immediately following the close of chapter 4's legislation for the common person's sin offering. The transition is marked within the three-column Masoretic layout without a strong parasha break, reflecting the close structural continuity between chapters 4 and 5 in the MT: both deal with the sin offering (chattat), and 5:1–13 extends the circumstances in which the chattat is required beyond the "unintentional sin" framework of chapter 4 to include cases of failed witness, contamination by uncleanness, and rash oaths.

The layout of the page makes clear the graduated structure of 5:1–13: the three opening case-conditions (vv. 1, 2–3, 4) are followed by the confession-and-offering injunction (vv. 5–6), then the two-tier graduated scale based on means (vv. 7–10 for the poor, vv. 11–13 for the very poor). The repetition of the structural closing formula וְנִסְלַח לוֹ ("and he shall be forgiven") is clearly visible at vv. 10 and 13, bracketing the graduated offering section.

Folio 60r carries the shift in the chapter at vv. 14–19 to the guilt offering (asham) legislation. The word אָשָׁם (asham) becomes visually dominant from v. 15 onward, clearly distinguishing this section from the chattat material of vv. 1–13. The chapter closes with the striking phrase: אָשַׁם אָשַׁם לַיהוָה ("asham asham la-YHWH" — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD"), the absolute infinitive construction of v. 19 which is one of the most emphatic phrasings in the entire chapter.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 89–90 (LXX, pa_0089–pa_0090).** Pages 89 and 90 were examined directly. The Vaticanus text on these pages carries the LXX text of Leviticus 5 in the characteristic fourth-century uncial hand in three continuous columns without word division. The chapter heading marking (CAP. XXXIII or equivalent) is visible in the outer margin of page 89. The Vaticanus pages show no significant lacunae or damage in the Leviticus 5 columns.

The most striking structural difference between the MT of Leviticus 5 and the LXX as witnessed in Vaticanus concerns what English Bibles number as Leviticus 6:1–7 (= MT Leviticus 5:20–26). In the MT the chapter extends to 26 verses; in the standard English numbering (following the LXX chapter division tradition), the chapter ends at v. 19 and 6:1–7 constitutes a separate unit. The translation here follows the MT 19-verse chapter, as specified in the task brief. The LXX column of page 89 confirms the beginning of this material and allows verification of the Greek rendering of the key terms asham, ma'al, chamishitho, and shekel ha-qodesh.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Ve-shama qol alah / "hears a public adjuration to testify" (v. 1).** Hebrew וְשָׁמַע קוֹל אָלָה (ve-shama qol alah) is one of the most discussed phrases in Leviticus 5 for its translation. Literally it reads "and he hears the voice of an oath/adjuration" — specifically a qol alah, the "voice/sound of an oath" which in context is a public summons to testify, a solemn adjuration by a judicial authority calling witnesses to come forward. The alah ("oath, adjuration, curse") here functions as a formal legal summons: an official has pronounced an oath-formula summoning anyone with relevant knowledge to testify. The person who has seen or knows something but refuses to come forward and speak up (lo yaggid) "shall bear his iniquity" — his silence constitutes a sin of omission.

   The phrase is difficult because alah can mean both "oath" (sworn by a party) and "adjuration/curse" (pronounced upon anyone who withholds testimony). Most modern translations take it as the latter: "if anyone sins in that he hears a public adjuration" (ESV) or "if a person sins in that he hears a public adjuration to testify" (NASB). This translation follows ESV/NASB in rendering it as a public adjuration to testify, which correctly captures the forensic setting. "Though he is a witness — whether he has seen or has come to know of it" renders the Hebrew אוֹ רָאָה אוֹ יָדָע (o ra'ah o yada', "either he saw or he knew") — the two sources of personal knowledge of a matter: direct eyewitness or indirect acquired knowledge. The LXX renders this passage with μάρτυς ("witness") and the phrase renders קוֹל אָלָה as φωνὴν ὁρκισμοῦ ("the voice/sound of an adjuration"), in close agreement with the forensic interpretation.

2. **Ve-hitvaddah / "he shall confess" (v. 5).** Hebrew וְהִתְוַדָּה (ve-hitvaddah, hithpael of ידה, yadah) — "and he shall confess." This is the first appearance of the hithpael of yadah in the context of confession in Leviticus, and it is programmatically significant. The hithpael reflexive form denotes the act of making a public verbal acknowledgment of one's own transgression. Confession (hitvaddah) precedes sacrifice and is integral to its efficacy: the offering without confession is incomplete. The verbal root yadah in hithpael can also carry the sense of "give thanks/praise," but in this forensic-cultic context the confessional sense is primary and unambiguous.

   "He shall confess" (ESV, NASB, KJV) is the standard rendering. The LXX renders ἐξαγορεύσει ("shall declare openly, shall confess"), using a compound of ἀγορεύω ("to speak publicly in the assembly") — the word choice emphasizes the public, declaratory character of the confession. This translation renders ve-hitvaddah as "he shall confess," adding the object "the sin he has committed" (asher chata aleiha) to complete the clause clearly.

3. **Asham / "guilt offering" (vv. 6–7, 14–19, throughout).** Hebrew אָשָׁם (asham) is the third major Levitical sacrifice introduced in the book, after the olah (burnt offering, ch. 1) and the chattat (sin offering, ch. 4). The asham (guilt offering, or reparation offering) is specifically tied to violations that involve a breach of trust, misappropriation of something belonging to another (including God's holy things), or cases where the precise nature of the sin is unknown but the feeling of guilt (asham) is present. The asham typically requires monetary restitution in addition to sacrifice — uniquely in the Levitical system.

   The word asham is used in this chapter in two overlapping senses: (a) as the name of the sacrifice itself (the "guilt offering") and (b) as the state of having incurred guilt (being "guilty"). This double usage is present in v. 19 where the phrase אָשַׁם אָשַׁם לַיהוָה (asham asham la-YHWH) uses the absolute infinitive with the finite verb: "he has certainly/indeed incurred guilt before the LORD." This translation renders the technical sacrificial term as "guilt offering" (ESV, NASB, KJV) throughout and renders the verbal/nominal guilt-state accordingly.

   Note that in vv. 6–7 the animal brought for the cases of vv. 1–4 is called both the "guilt offering" (asham) and "sin offering" (chattat) — v. 6 calls the lamb or goat an asham, but names it chattat; v. 7 explicitly calls the turtledoves one for chattat and one for olah. This has generated extensive scholarly discussion. The most defensible interpretation is that the asham here in vv. 6–7 refers to the state of guilt incurred (not to a distinct guilt offering) and the actual sacrifice prescribed is the chattat. This translation renders v. 6 as "he shall bring his guilt offering" (using asham in its guilt-liability sense) while identifying the animal as "a sin offering" (chattat), following the MT structure.

   The LXX renders asham throughout with περὶ τῆς πλημμελείας αὐτοῦ ("concerning his trespass/offense"), using πλημμέλεια ("a false note, an offense, a trespass") — a somewhat unusual word choice that captures the sense of going wrong or out of tune. For the guilt offering as sacrifice, the LXX uses κριὸν ἄμωμον ("a ram without blemish") in v. 15 as the offering itself, in agreement with the MT.

4. **Ma'al / "unfaithfulness" (v. 15).** Hebrew מַעַל (ma'al) is a significant theological term denoting an act of breach of faith, treacherous violation of a trust relationship. In the cultic context of v. 15 it describes the specific sin against "the holy things of the LORD" (qodshe YHWH): misappropriating or trespassing on what belongs to God — consuming a tithe without proper accounting, or using holy property for common purposes. Ma'al is a strong term: it is used elsewhere of Israel's unfaithfulness to the covenant (Josh 7:1; 1 Chr 9:1; Ezek 14:13). In v. 15 the combination ma'al bi-shgagah ("commits unfaithfulness unintentionally") reflects the unique character of this asham case: the ma'al may be inadvertent, yet it is still a genuine breach of the sacred trust relationship.

   "Unfaithfulness" (ESV) is the standard rendering. "Trespass" (KJV, older tradition) captures the territorial violation aspect; "breach of faith" (NJPS) captures the relational trust aspect. This translation uses "unfaithfulness" with "acts of unfaithfulness" for the verbal construction ma'al ma'al (v. 15: "commits an act of unfaithfulness"), preserving the emphatic double-root construction of the MT. The LXX renders ma'al with πλημμελήσῃ ("shall commit a trespass/false step"), from the same πλημμελ- root used for asham — the LXX treats ma'al and asham as semantically aligned violations.

5. **Chamishitho / "a fifth" (v. 16).** Hebrew חֲמִישִׁתוֹ (chamishitho) — "its fifth" or "a fifth of it" — the restitution penalty above and beyond the principal. When someone has committed ma'al against the holy things, he must restore the full principal value AND add twenty percent (a fifth) on top of it. This one-fifth surcharge (chamesh, from the root for "five") is the restitution premium specific to the asham legislation and distinguishes it sharply from the chattat, which requires no monetary restitution. The same one-fifth principle appears in Lev 22:14 (an inadvertent eating of holy food) and Lev 27:13–31 (vow redemptions).

   "A fifth" (ESV, NASB, KJV) renders chamishitho literally. Some translations render it "twenty percent" for clarity. This translation follows the formal "add a fifth to it," which matches ESV and preserves the direct quantitative language of the MT. The LXX renders τὸ ἐπίπεμπτον ("the fifth-above," i.e., the added fifth), using a compound that precisely captures the surplus-addition sense of the Hebrew.

6. **Shekel ha-qodesh / "the shekel of the sanctuary" (v. 15).** Hebrew שֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ (shekel ha-qodesh) — "the shekel of holiness / sanctuary shekel." This is a specific weight standard maintained by the sanctuary for evaluating offerings and payments to the LORD. In the ancient Near East weight standards varied by region, trade guild, and institution; the "sanctuary shekel" (cf. Exod 30:13) was the official standard of the sacred precinct, distinct from commercial shekel standards in circulation. Its use here ensures that the valuation of the guilt offering ram is calculated by the authoritative sacred standard, not by any potentially debased commercial measure.

   "The shekel of the sanctuary" (ESV, NASB, KJV) is the standard formal rendering and is used throughout the Leviticus series. "According to the sanctuary standard" (NIV) is a functional paraphrase. The LXX renders τῷ σίκλῳ τῷ ἁγίῳ ("by the holy shekel"), in close agreement with the MT.

7. **"Valued by you in silver shekels" (v. 15).** The phrase בְּעֶרְכְּךָ כֶּסֶף שְׁקָלִים (be-erkekha keseph sheqalim — "according to your valuation, silver shekels") establishes that the priest is to assign a monetary value to the guilt offering ram. The erakh ("valuation") is the official cultic assessment of value and is the governing concept of Leviticus 27's redemption schedules. Here in 5:15 it is applied to the guilt offering ram: the offerer brings a ram whose silver value, reckoned by the priest according to the sanctuary shekel standard, serves as a measure of the gravity of the sin against holy things. "Valued by you" follows ESV in rendering be-erkekha as addressed to the priest (the second-person singular "you" referring to the priestly assessor). Some render "in your estimation" (NASB: "according to your valuation"). This translation renders "valued by you in silver shekels, according to the shekel of the sanctuary" to preserve the full syntax of the phrase.

8. **"Though he did not know it" (vv. 17–18).** Verse 17 presents the most difficult case in chapter 5: a person who has a feeling of guilt (or who suspects he may have sinned) but does not know specifically what violation has occurred. The phrase לֹא יָדַע (lo yada' — "and he did not know") in v. 17, following וְלֹא יָדַע (ve-lo yada') in v. 18, creates a double emphasis on the unknowing character of the violation. Yet the asham is still required: the subjective experience of guilt triggers the offering even in the absence of specific knowledge of the sin.

   This case has been called the "floating guilt" or "suspensive asham" in rabbinic and modern scholarship. It signals that the Levitical guilt offering addresses not only known violations but also the morally significant state of uncertain guilt. "Though he did not know it" (ESV, NASB) renders lo yada' accurately. The LXX renders καὶ οὐκ ᾔδει ("and he did not know"), in direct agreement.

9. **"He shall bear his iniquity" (vv. 1, 17).** Hebrew וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono — "and he shall bear/carry his iniquity"). The phrase nasa avon ("to bear iniquity/guilt") is a forensic-theological expression describing the state of being held accountable for a sin — carrying the legal and moral weight of a violation. In v. 1 it describes the consequence of failed testimony: the silent witness who does not come forward bears his iniquity (perhaps with the sense that the guilt falls back on him rather than being expiated). In v. 17 it describes the state of the person with floating guilt who is yet to bring the asham.

   "Shall bear his iniquity" (ESV, NASB) renders nasa avon literally and forcefully. The LXX renders λήμψεται τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ("shall receive/take up the sin"), which is a somewhat unusual rendering of nasa — the verb λαμβάνω ("to take, receive") renders the "carrying" sense of nasa, but the direction of the metaphor shifts slightly: rather than bearing a weight, the LXX frames it as receiving sin upon oneself.

10. **"The memorial portion" (v. 12).** Hebrew אֶת-אַזְכָּרָתָהּ (et-azkaratah — "its memorial portion" or "its token portion"). The azkarah (from zakar, "to remember") is the handful of grain offering that is burned on the altar as a "reminder" or "representative portion" on behalf of the whole offering. The concept is that the burning handful on the altar constitutes a cultic act of remembrance before the LORD, while the remainder of the grain goes to the priest. For the very poor offerer's flour sin offering (v. 11–13), the azkarah is burned on the altar "on the fire offerings to the LORD" (al-isheh ha-YHWH). "Memorial portion" (ESV, NASB) is the standard formal rendering. The LXX renders τὸ μνημόσυνον αὐτῆς ("its memorial / its remembrance"), in close agreement.

11. **"The prescribed rule" / "according to the rule" (v. 10).** Hebrew כַּמִּשְׁפָּט (ka-mishpat — "according to the rule/custom/ordinance"). Verse 10's instruction that the second bird is to be offered as a burnt offering ka-mishpat ("according to the prescribed rule") refers back to the burnt offering procedures of Leviticus 1:14–17 (the bird burnt offering). The mishpat is the established procedural norm. "According to the prescribed rule" (ESV) renders ka-mishpat clearly. NASB uses "according to the ordinance." The LXX renders κατὰ τὴν κρίσιν ("according to the judgment/ordinance"), using κρίσις for mishpat — close agreement.

12. **"He shall wring its head from its neck but shall not sever it" (v. 8).** Hebrew וּמָלַק אֶת-רֹאשׁוֹ מִמּוּל עָרְפּוֹ וְלֹא יַבְדִּיל (u-malaq et-rosho mi-mul orpo ve-lo yavdil — "and he shall wring the head from opposite its nape/back-of-neck and shall not divide/sever it"). The verb malaq (to wring/nip) describes the technique for killing a bird offering: the priest pinches and twists the head at the back of the neck (oref), breaking the neck without completely severing the head. This technique is prescribed for the sin offering bird (v. 8) while the burnt offering bird (Lev 1:15) uses the same verb but may involve a different degree of severing. The phrase "shall not sever it" (lo yavdil) distinguishes this from a complete decapitation. "He shall wring its head from its neck but shall not sever it" renders the full phrase accurately. The LXX renders ἀποκνίσει τὴν κεφαλὴν αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ τοῦ τραχήλου ("shall pinch off the head from the throat/neck"), in close agreement.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 89–90, pa_0089–pa_0090) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5:1 | qol alah ("voice/sound of an adjuration") | φωνὴν ὁρκισμοῦ ("the voice of an adjuration/oath") | LXX renders alah with ὁρκισμός ("adjuration, binding oath"), a judicial-forensic term. Close agreement with the MT's forensic sense. |
| 5:1 | ve-shama ("and he hears") | ἀκούσῃ ("if he hears / when he hears") | LXX uses the aorist subjunctive for the conditional, accurately rendering the conditional clause structure of the MT. |
| 5:1 | lo yaggid ("does not speak/tell") | μὴ ἀπαγγείλῃ ("does not report/declare") | LXX uses ἀπαγγέλλω ("to report, bring a message, declare formally") for higgid (hiphil of nagad), giving the act of testimony a formal declaratory character — appropriate for a legal witness before an authority. |
| 5:2–3 | nefesh ("person/soul") | ψυχή ("soul / person") | LXX renders nefesh with ψυχή throughout, as is its consistent practice. Close agreement. |
| 5:4 | le-hara o le-heitiv ("to do evil or to do good") | κακοποιῆσαι ἢ καλοποιῆσαι ("to do wickedly or to do well") | LXX renders the pair precisely; the Greek verbal pair κακοποιῆσαι / καλοποιῆσαι mirrors the Hebrew idiom. Full agreement. |
| 5:5 | ve-hitvaddah ("and he shall confess") | ἐξαγορεύσει ("shall openly declare / shall confess") | LXX uses ἐξαγορεύω, a compound emphasizing public, formal declaration. Somewhat stronger than the MT's hithpael reflexive, but semantically aligned. |
| 5:6 | asham ("guilt offering / guilt") | περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας αὐτοῦ ("concerning his sin") | LXX renders asham (in its guilt-liability sense in vv. 6–7) as περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας, folding the asham concept into the sin-offering (chattat) language. The LXX does not sharply distinguish asham from chattat in vv. 6–7, which complicates the verse's structure — but since the animal is named chattat in the same verse, the LXX rendering is pragmatically coherent. |
| 5:7 | ve-im lo taggiah yado ("if his hand does not reach / if he cannot afford") | ἐὰν δὲ μὴ ἰσχύῃ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτοῦ ("if his hand is not strong enough") | LXX renders the idiom of "hand not reaching" (taggiah, from naga') with the literal "hand not being strong enough" (μὴ ἰσχύῃ), capturing the sense of financial inability. Close semantic agreement. |
| 5:9 | ve-hizzah mi-dam ha-chattat ("and he shall sprinkle some of the blood of the sin offering") | προσρανεῖ ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος τοῦ περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ("shall sprinkle from the blood of the [offering] concerning sin") | LXX uses the same sprinkling verb προσρανεῖ as in Lev 4:6, 17, consistent with its rendering of hizzah throughout. |
| 5:11 | lo-yasim aleha shemen ("he shall put no oil on it") | οὐκ ἐπιχεεῖ ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸ ἔλαιον ("shall not pour oil upon it") | LXX uses ἐπιχέω ("to pour upon") for sim ("put/place"), adding a pouring connotation to what the MT describes as simply placing oil. The absence of oil is an austerity marker for the poorest sin offering. LXX agrees with MT that oil is excluded. |
| 5:11 | ve-lo yiten aleha levonah ("and shall not place frankincense on it") | οὐδὲ ἐπιθήσει ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸ λίβανον ("nor shall he place frankincense upon it") | Direct agreement. Frankincense (levonah / λίβανος) and oil are both excluded from the poorest sin offering, distinguishing it from normal grain offerings which receive both (Lev 2:1). |
| 5:12 | al-isheh ha-YHWH ("on the fire offerings of the LORD") | ἐπὶ τὰ κάρπωμα Κυρίου ("upon the burnt offerings of the LORD") | LXX renders isheh with κάρπωμα ("fruit offering / burnt offering gift"), its standard rendering for isheh. This diverges from the literal fire-derivation of isheh but is consistent across the LXX Leviticus. See also Lev 1:9, 3:11, 4:35 notes. This translation retains "fire offerings of the LORD." |
| 5:15 | ma'al ("unfaithfulness / breach of faith") | πλημμελήσῃ / πλημμελείας ("to trespass / trespass") | LXX uses πλημμελέω / πλημμέλεια throughout for both ma'al and asham, treating both as denoting a "false step" or "going wrong." This lexical merger in the LXX somewhat flattens the MT's distinction between ma'al (breach of trust) and asham (guilt, guilt offering). |
| 5:15 | be-erkekha ("according to your valuation") | ἐκτιμήσει ("shall estimate / shall value") | LXX renders erkekha (from erekh, "valuation") with ἐκτιμάω ("to appraise, to estimate"), accurately reflecting the priestly valuation role. The use of a third-person verb ("shall value") rather than the MT's second-person "according to your valuation" slightly shifts the subject from priest-as-assessor to a more general statement. |
| 5:15 | shekel ha-qodesh ("the shekel of the sanctuary") | τῷ σίκλῳ τῷ ἁγίῳ ("the holy shekel") | LXX renders ha-qodesh with τῷ ἁγίῳ ("the holy"), in direct agreement with the MT's construct chain. The σίκλος is the standard LXX transliteration of shekel. |
| 5:16 | chamishitho ("its fifth / a fifth of it") | τὸ ἐπίπεμπτον ("the added fifth") | LXX renders chamishitho with ἐπίπεμπτον, a compound of ἐπί ("upon, added") + πέμπτος ("fifth") — "the additional fifth." This precisely captures the surplus-addition nature of chamishitho. Full agreement. |
| 5:17 | bi-shgagah ("unintentionally") | ἀκουσίως ("unwittingly / involuntarily") | LXX renders bi-shgagah with ἀκουσίως, consistent with its rendering in Lev 4:2, 13, 22, 27. |
| 5:19 | asham asham la-YHWH ("he has indeed incurred guilt before the LORD") | πλημμελείᾳ ἐπλημμέλησεν ἔναντι Κυρίου ("by a trespass he has trespassed before the LORD") | LXX renders the MT's absolute infinitive construction (asham asham — emphatic: "he has certainly been guilty") with the cognate dative + verb construction πλημμελείᾳ ἐπλημμέλησεν, a Greek calque of the Hebrew emphatic-infinitive idiom. The Greek construction precisely mirrors the Hebrew rhetorical device. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 5)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| אָשָׁם (asham) | guilt offering (sacrifice); incurred guilt (state) | Distinguishes the sacrificial offering from the guilt-state; in vv. 6–7 asham = guilt liability, chattat = the actual offering |
| מַעַל (ma'al) | unfaithfulness | Breach of sacred trust; LXX: πλημμέλεια |
| וְהִתְוַדָּה (ve-hitvaddah) | he shall confess | Hithpael of yadah; confession precedes and is integral to the sacrifice; LXX: ἐξαγορεύσει |
| חֲמִישִׁתוֹ (chamishitho) | a fifth (add a fifth) | Twenty percent restitution surcharge unique to the asham; LXX: ἐπίπεמπτον |
| שֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ (shekel ha-qodesh) | shekel of the sanctuary | Official sanctuary weight standard; LXX: σίκλος ἅγιος |
| אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) | memorial portion | The handful burned on the altar; LXX: μνημόσυνον |
| וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (ve-nasa avono) | he shall bear his iniquity | Forensic expression: the unexipiated guilt rests on the offender; LXX: λήμψεται τὴν ἁμαρτίαν |
| מָלַק (malaq) | wring (the head) | Bird-killing technique: wringing the neck without severing; LXX: ἀποκνίζω |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established from Lev 4; continues through ch. 5 |
| כִּפֶּר (kipper) | make atonement | Established from Lev 1:4; piel; LXX: ἐξιλάσεται |
| וְנִסְלַח (ve-nishlach) | and he shall be forgiven | Established from Lev 4; niphal salach; LXX: ἀφεθήσεται |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Established from Lev 1; LXX: κάρπωμα |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1 |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from Lev 2 |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד | tent of meeting | Established convention |
| עֶשְׂרוֹן סֹלֶת (isaron solet) | a tenth of an ephah of fine flour | Standard measure for the poorest grain-type offering |

— LOGOS
